perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California might create conflicts if the contract terms violate specific California labor laws or other regulations, which differ significantly from Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the Services Agreement involves employees performing services in California, changing the governing law to California would mandate compliance with CA Labor Code § 201 regarding timely payment of wages upon termination. The original Pennsylvania law may have different regulations and timelines which will contradict CA law.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by switching the governing law from Pennsylvania to California, introduces a potential contradiction regarding wage payment upon termination, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1. California Labor Code § 201(a) mandates immediate wage payment upon discharge. If the original Pennsylvania law has different regulations, the changed text could lead to a direct violation of California law if an employee performing services in California is not paid immediately upon termination, thus creating a contradiction. The contradiction is impactful because it directly concerns compliance with a specific labor law.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall be effective as of the date first above written upon its execution and delivery by each of the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall be effective 90 days after the date first above written upon its execution and delivery by each of the parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the effective date by 90 days could conflict with regulatory requirements if the services being provided are subject to specific deadlines or reporting requirements under the Investment Company Act of 1940. For example, certain SEC filings or compliance obligations have strict timelines and a delayed effective date might create non-compliance if those deadlines are missed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 30b1-1 requires investment companies to file Form N-SAR semiannually. A delayed effective date may hinder the ability to gather the information required to file in a timely manner.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day delay to the effective date of the amendment. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate this could conflict with Rule 30b1-1 and other regulatory requirements due to strict timelines. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 1 is related to website access, the explanations provided point to a potential contradiction with compliance deadlines, which falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, specifically where a time-sensitive obligation in a contract does not align with legal requirements, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Employees of Federated Advisory Services Company provide support to portfolio managers and other employees of affiliated advisers.
perturbation.changed_text: Employees of Federated Advisory Services Company may provide support to portfolio managers and other employees of affiliated advisers at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'provide support' to 'may provide support at their discretion' introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance, especially if the services outlined are critical for regulatory compliance. This discretionary language might conflict with the Adviser's obligations under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 to provide adequate and consistent advisory services.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 prohibits investment advisers from engaging in fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. If the support services being discretionary leads to inadequate oversight or misrepresentation of services, this can lead to violation of the law.
perturbation.location: 1. Amendment to Exhibit A to Services Agreement

Prohibited transactions by investment advisers It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, directly or indirectly- (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce , directly or indirectly— (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which operates as a fraud or deceit upon any client or prospective client; (3) acting as principal for his own account, knowingly to sell any security to or purchase any security from a client, or acting as broker for a person other than such client, knowingly to effect any sale or purchase of any security for the account of such client, without disclosing to such client in writing before the completion of such transaction the capacity in which he is acting and obtaining the consent of the client to such transaction. The prohibitions of this paragraph shall not apply to any transaction with a customer of a broker or dealer if such broker or dealer is not acting as an investment adviser in relation to such transaction; or (4) to engage in any act, practice, or course of business which is fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. The Commission shall, for the purposes of this paragraph (4) by rules and regulations define, and prescribe means reasonably designed to prevent, such acts, practices, and courses of business as are fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. (Aug. 22, 1940, ch. 686, title II, § 206, 54 Stat. 852 ; Pub. L. 86–750 , §§ 8, 9, Sept. 13, 1960 , 74 Stat. 887 ; Pub. L. 111–203, title IX, § 985(e)(2) , July 21, 2010 , 124 Stat. 1935 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2010—Par. (3). Pub. L. 111–203 inserted “or” at end. 1960— Pub. L.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces discretionary language ('may provide support at their discretion') which weakens the obligation compared to the original text ('provide support'). This shift potentially contradicts Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, as outlined in both scraped snippets, which prohibits investment advisers from engaging in fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. If the support services are essential for compliance and are provided at the employee's discretion, this could lead to inadequate oversight or misrepresentation of services. This discretionary action is in conflict with ensuring that services are consistent and adequate which would be a violation of 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 which states it is unlawful to engage in any practice that operates as a fraud or deceit upon a client. Making the support services optional introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance where the Act requires a higher standard of care.
